funding_source: No external funding. Internal research project.
co_investigators: Jaycee Bennett (Co-Investigator, Student Researcher). Contact: [email to be added]
citi_training_completion: Principal Investigator (Dr. Christopher Castille): CITI training in Human Subjects Research (Social/Behavioral Research). Co-Investigator (Jaycee Bennett): Human Subjects Research - Student Researchers (Basic Course), completed 11-Jan-2025, expires 11-Jan-2027, Record ID 66618363.
protocol_description: This study examines whether goals operate as reference points (Heath et al., 1999; Larrick et al., 2009): when people fall short of a goal, they may become more risk-seeking and more willing to endorse ethically questionable strategies to "hit the goal." The study has two parts: (1) Replication: Direct replications of classic vignette problems where participants make choices between sure vs. risky options depending on whether they are described as below or above a goal; (2) Extension: Novel scenarios where participants judge how acceptable another person's behavior is when that person is below vs. above a goal.
population_description: Participants will be undergraduate students enrolled in Management and Marketing and related courses at Nicholls State University. Participants must be at least 18 years of age. There are no specific exclusion criteria beyond age requirements. Participants will be recruited through the PRAMS system and may receive course credit for participation.
research_procedures:
The study involves the following procedures:

1. Informed Consent: Participants will read and provide electronic consent before beginning the study.

2. Demographics and Background: Brief demographic questions if needed for analyses.

3. Vignette Block: Participants are randomly assigned 3–6 vignettes from a pool. Each vignette presents a short scenario (2–4 sentences) and asks either: (a) Binary choice: "Which would you choose? (A) [sure option] or (B) [risky option]"; or (b) Likert rating: "How acceptable was [person]'s decision? 1 = Not at all acceptable … 7 = Completely acceptable."

4. Attention Checks: 1–2 items (e.g., "Select 'Strongly agree' to show you are paying attention") to ensure data quality.

5. Optional: End-of-survey question asking whether participants would like to receive a summary of findings when the study is complete (with optional email).

6. Debriefing: Participants receive a brief explanation of the study aims and hypotheses, including that the study tests whether goals (as reference points) influence risk-taking and judgments about ethically gray behavior.

All data collection will occur online through Qualtrics or equivalent survey platform hosted on Nicholls State University servers. The entire procedure takes approximately 15–25 minutes to complete.
research_objectives:
The primary objectives of this research are:

1. To replicate classic vignette problems (Heath et al., 1999; Larrick et al., 2009; Au & Feldman, 2020) where participants make choices between sure vs. risky options depending on goal status
2. To extend the goals-as-reference-points mechanism to vignette-based judgments about unethical behavior (ethical gray choices, motivated communication, deception permissibility)
3. To test whether participants are more risk-seeking when below vs. above a goal (loss vs. gain domain)
4. To test whether participants report greater acceptability of ethically questionable strategies when the actor is below vs. above goal
5. To contribute to scientific understanding of goals, reference points, and ethical decision-making
research_questions:
1. Will participants be more risk-seeking when below the goal (loss domain) than when above (gain domain)?
2. Will participants exhibit diminishing sensitivity: changes near the goal having larger effects than equivalent changes far from the goal?
3. Will participants report greater acceptability of ethically questionable strategies when the actor is below vs. above goal?
4. Will participants report greater deception permissibility when the scenario involves high ambiguity/elasticity, especially combined with goal shortfall?
5. Will participants attribute unethical behavior more to goal pressure when the actor is below vs. above goal?
educational_justification:
This research serves educational purposes by:
- Providing students with hands-on experience in behavioral research on decision-making
- Teaching students about goals as reference points and Prospect Theory
- Demonstrating research methodology and vignette-based data collection
- Contributing to students' understanding of ethical decision-making in organizational contexts
recruitment_method: Participants will be recruited through the Participant Recruitment and Management System (PRAMS) platform. Students enrolled at Nicholls State University courses will be able to view and sign up for this study through the PRAMS system. Recruitment materials will be posted on the PRAMS system with a clear description of the study requirements, duration, and credit value.
recruitment_script:
Study Title: Goals as Reference Points and Risk Taking

Description: This study examines how goals influence decision-making and risk-taking. You will complete 3–6 short scenarios in which you either make choices (e.g., between a sure outcome vs. a risky option) or rate how acceptable another person's behavior is. Each scenario takes about 1–2 minutes.

Duration: Approximately 15–25 minutes
Potential Credit Value: 0.5 credit (to participating courses)
Mode: Online (complete at your own pace)

Requirements: Must be 18 years of age or older
inclusion_criteria:
- Must be at least 18 years of age
- Must be currently enrolled as a student at Nicholls State University
- Must be enrolled in a course that offers research participation credit through PRAMS
- Must have access to a computer or device with internet connection
- Must be fluent in English and able to complete an online survey
exclusion_criteria:
- Under 18 years of age
- Not currently enrolled at Nicholls State University
- Unable to complete online surveys due to technical limitations
- Previously participated in this study (to avoid duplicate responses)
benefits_to_subjects:
Participants will benefit from:
- Potentially receiving course credit for participation (0.5 credit)
- Contributing to scientific knowledge about goals and decision-making
- Optional summary of findings upon study completion (if they provide email)
- Learning about goals as reference points and ethical decision-making through the debriefing
benefits_to_others:
This research may benefit:
- Other students and researchers interested in goals, reference points, and ethical decision-making
- Educators developing training on ethical behavior in organizations
- Organizations seeking to understand how goals influence ethical choices
benefits_to_society:
This research contributes to:
- Scientific understanding of goals as reference points and their effects on risk-taking and ethical judgments
- Understanding of ethical decision-making in organizational contexts
- Development of better theories and interventions related to goal-setting and ethical behavior
payment_compensation: Participants may receive 0.5 course credit through the PRAMS system for their participation. No monetary compensation will be provided.
costs_to_subjects: The only cost to participants is their time (approximately 15–25 minutes). There are no financial costs, physical risks, or other burdens associated with participation.
review_type_justification:
This study qualifies for exempt status under Category D (Educational settings, anonymous surveys/interviews) because:

1. The study involves minimal risk to participants (online vignette scenarios and ratings)
2. All data collection is anonymous – participants are identified only by PRAMS ID numbers
3. The study involves standard educational and psychological assessment procedures
4. No sensitive topics or vulnerable populations are involved beyond hypothetical ethically gray scenarios (participants judge another person's actions; they do not make unethical choices themselves)
5. Participants are adults (18+) who can provide informed consent
6. The study does not involve deception, physical procedures, or collection of sensitive information
7. All procedures are standard in educational research settings

The study involves only anonymous data collection through online surveys, with no collection of identifying information beyond PRAMS ID numbers.
risk_statement:
This study involves minimal risk to participants. The primary potential risks are:

1. Sensitive content: Some extension vignettes describe ethically gray behavior (e.g., expense inflation, misreporting sales). Participants judge another person's actions; they do not make unethical choices themselves. Scenarios are common in organizational/behavioral ethics research.

2. Time commitment: Participants invest approximately 15–25 minutes of their time, but this is compensated with course credit.

3. Privacy concerns: While all data is anonymized, there is a minimal risk of data breach. However, no personally identifying information is collected beyond PRAMS ID numbers.

The risks in this study are no greater than those encountered in normal daily activities or routine educational assessments.
risk_mitigation:
Risks are mitigated through:

1. Comprehensive debriefing: All participants receive debriefing explaining the study purpose, hypotheses, and that there is no deception.

2. Anonymization: All data is collected with PRAMS ID numbers only – no names, email addresses, or other identifying information is collected (unless participant voluntarily provides email for findings summary).

3. Voluntary participation: Participants can withdraw at any time without penalty.

4. Secure data storage: All data is stored securely on password-protected servers with access limited to the research team.

5. Contact information: Participants are provided with PI and IRB contact information for questions or concerns.
data_collection_methods:
Data will be collected through Qualtrics (or equivalent) survey platform using the following methods:

1. Vignette responses: Binary choices or Likert ratings for each assigned vignette
2. Attention check responses: 1–2 items to ensure data quality
3. Optional demographics: Brief demographic questions if needed
4. Optional email: For participants who wish to receive a summary of findings

All data collection occurs through secure, encrypted online forms. Data is stored with PRAMS ID numbers as the only identifier. Assignment of vignettes is done via embedded data or URL parameters.
data_storage: All data will be stored securely on password-protected servers maintained by Nicholls State University. Data will be stored in the study database, which uses industry-standard encryption and security measures. Access to data is restricted to the principal investigator and authorized research team members. Data will be stored with PRAMS ID numbers only – no personally identifying information (names, email addresses, etc.) will be stored with the research data.
confidentiality_procedures:
Confidentiality is maintained through:

1. Anonymization: Participants are identified only by PRAMS ID numbers, which are already anonymized in the PRAMS system. No names, email addresses, or other identifying information is collected or stored with research data (unless participant voluntarily provides email for findings summary, which is stored separately).

2. Secure access: Only the principal investigator and authorized research team members have access to the data, and all access is logged and monitored.

3. Data encryption: All data is stored using industry-standard encryption methods.

4. Limited data sharing: Data will only be shared in aggregated, anonymized form for publication purposes. Individual participant data will be shared but in anonymized form.

5. IRB compliance: All procedures follow IRB guidelines for data confidentiality and security.
data_retention: Research data will be retained for a minimum of 5 years after study completion, as required by IRB regulations. After this period, data may be retained longer for potential follow-up analyses or publication purposes, but will continue to be stored securely and confidentially. Anonymized data/analyses will be made available to the Open Science Framework.
data_access:
Access to research data is restricted to:
- Principal Investigator (Dr. Christopher Castille)
- Authorized research team members (if any)
- IRB members for review purposes (if requested)

Data will be accessed only for research analysis, publication preparation, and IRB compliance. All data access is logged and monitored.
consent_procedures:
Informed consent will be obtained electronically through the PRAMS system (or survey platform) before participants begin the study. The consent process includes:

1. Consent form display: Participants will see a comprehensive consent form explaining the study purpose, procedures, risks, benefits, and their rights as participants.

2. Consent questions: Participants must answer questions confirming they understand the study and their rights before proceeding.

3. Electronic consent: Participants provide consent by clicking "Yes, I consent to participate" after reading the consent form. They can also click on an "opt out" button and exit the study without penalty.

4. Right to withdraw: The consent form clearly states that participants can withdraw at any time without penalty.

5. Contact information: The consent form includes contact information for the principal investigator and IRB for questions or concerns.

The consent form is stored electronically as part of the research record.
data_monitoring_plan: Data will be monitored throughout the study period to ensure data quality, appropriate participant recruitment, and compliance with IRB protocols. The principal investigator will review data collection progress and address any issues that arise. Sequential Bayesian inference may be used; vignettes may be retired when evidence reaches a threshold. This adaptive design does not affect individual participants' experience.
oversight_procedures: Study oversight will be provided by the Principal Investigator and IRB. Any adverse events or protocol deviations will be reported to the IRB immediately.
publication_plan: Results from this study may be prepared for publication. All publications will use aggregated, anonymized data only.
data_sharing_plan: Individual participant data will be shared but in anonymized form. Aggregated data may be shared through publication and open science repositories.
participant_access_to_results: Participants who optionally provide email may receive a summary of findings when the study is complete. Others can contact the principal investigator to request a summary.
appendices_notes: Supporting documents include: Vignette bank description; consent form; recruitment copy. Key citations: Heath et al. (1999); Larrick et al. (2009); Au & Feldman (2020); Schweitzer & Hsee (2002). All materials are available for IRB review upon request.
irb_contact_notes: For questions or concerns about this research, participants can contact the Principal Investigator or IRB Office (Dr. Alaina Daigle, 985-448-4697).
